item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting idea, blandly executed with a few really crucial errors that just murder it.

Critique
– hide block

◾Magic

I actually really dig this being on the list, because that was a SERIOUS ACCUSATION…centuries ago, which the article suggests directly. Third century BCE? Plenty of time for accusations about magic. Here's the problem.

The rest of the article suggests it took the Foundation a WHILE to fully determine its effects and how to account for them. It also explicitly states that the Foundation didn't get it at all until 20XX. How do they know that people have been accused of magic thanks to this? Or, frankly, a third of the rest of the list(apostasy, sodomy, blasphemy - even incest, depending on area). These are NOT modern criminal accusations. Bare minimum, there needs to be some mention of records going along with this describing history to account for this, or else it's just a huge and sloppy plot hole.

And I really have to echo the issues with that expunge. It's…it's bad. It contribute nothing, hints nothing, and is just a glaring middle finger to the reader. On top of that, the defenses shown by you as the Author are incredibly paltry and flimsy. If you're trying to cite the redaction guide, you're doing that entirely wrong - YOU, yourself, Author, need to know what it is, not leave it up to the reader to determine for you. Sloppy and lazy expunge. Remove it and the article does nothing but profit. There's enough latitude for 'potentially more crimes it could make happen' already baked into the first line of the addendum.

The following is a list of known accusations made against individuals who touched SCP-1453.

Don't shoot yourself in the foot when you've already got what you need set up there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sets up an interesting concept and idea but never goes anywhere with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must be a dry person because I liked this. The intricacy of the legal proceedings needed to go through people exposed to this I found to be hilariously over the top

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm wondering what would happen if someone just handled this thing with tongs. As it is,

Under no circumstances are any personnel to touch SCP-1453, even if wearing hazardous material gear.

could just refer to it working through gloves. While the robotic equipment would likely be safest, what would the effect be in the event of a prosthetic limb, some reach extending grasping device (eg. those grabbers they market for people with limited mobility, BBQ tongs, etc.), or just a stick being used to move this thing.

item.title: Necro post
item.content:encoded: Hate to necro, but this is a big turn off for me too. That final [EXPUNGED] No hint. No clue. Just expunged cause reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I think you're working from a terrifying real-world premise here, in the end the article doesn't become all that interesting. Not that I could tell you how to change that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's fine, these are open posts.

And while that is a scary thing to go through, this article doesn't really adequately pass that fear on. If there was maybe some kind of interview with the accused, something to give it a human touch, there would be some payoff, but there just isn't. It's boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is true that trials cost money and resources and can take a long time.

If it's an internal matter, than it is simply handled through "the relevant misconduct hearings." Trials only come into play if the case is handled first in an external judiciary (i.e., real world courts). The second trial - the one behind closed Foundation doors - is done because SCP-1453, by its very nature, throws into question the verdict of a court not aware of its existence. Accordingly, the circumstances are retried without prejudice (thus trying to avoid double jeopardy). The reason to not automatically give the accused a full pardon, no questions asked, is to prevent 1453 from being used as an excuse by someone who is, actually, legitimately guilty. Retrial without prejudice allows the case to be weighed fairly on its merits, with the understanding that some of the evidence was fabricated by an anomalous object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes you believe you've committed a crime, and then evidence of the crime appears?

Not at all. It causes you to be falsely accused of a crime you did not commit, and then evidence and witnesses inexplicably starts to appear supporting the accusations.

Without getting into any sort of detail, I can regrettably say from personal experience that being falsely accused of a crime is one of the more terrifying things an adult might encounter in the real world. Most modern Western justice systems have some variation of a presumption of innocence. It would be unfeasible to write up an SCP that warped a fundamental underpinning of a society such as the courts to take that away, so instead of changing the system, the object magically produces evidence strongly suggesting if not "proving" outright the guilt of the accused.

Pick a crime you think those guilty of ought to end up in a special circle of Hell - the sort of crime where at best the guilty party's life is over (even if they keep their freedom). Now, imagine yourself accused of said crime. Now, imagine that you found yourself in a set of circumstances wherein you had to try and prove your innocence, rather than have your accusers prove your guilt. That is what this does to you.

Edit: Reply to critique, not to Staff Post. Sorry bout that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with what Roget said here. I think that the containment procedures don't make sense; trials cost money, and wouldn't it be easier to just use authority to withdraw affected personnel?

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is up for review. Check it out, or it'll judge you.

This is pretty bland. It makes you believe you've committed a crime, and then evidence of the crime appears? It feels like a lot of thought went into this, but all the thought was into the parts of the idea I found the least interesting, like the containment procedures. The actual writing had some bits that bothered me, like the big block of text for the containment procedures, repetition of "circumstances" and "good standing", and the " determine if all allegations" bits. I also thought the list at the end was a really lame way to end, a bunch of crimes with shitty expungement. So, overall, this is a pretty boring idea that isn't built on at all and doesn't do anything interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is my canon too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes sense. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Performing magic or witchcraft was a capital offense in many societies until relatively recently. Definitely a way to have your reputation go through the floor if accused - it was also one of those offenses where there was generally a presumption of guilt until innocence could be proven. And proving yourself incapable of performing magic usually involved dying (if I toss you in a river and you don't drown, you're a witch; if I toss you in a river and you do drown, you aren't).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Magic? What do you mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<puts on authority hat relating to government redaction>

If something is expunged, it is deleted. That sheet of paper was run through the shredder and all copies were stuck in the furnace. Gone. NOBODY has access to it anymore.

The Foundation isn't a government.

It does have similarities to a government organization, but details like this are up to individual readers/writers. "Expunged" in different contexts can also mean "suppressed".

And in context of the Foundation, wiping information completely forever from existence is absolutely nonsensical. It goes in direct contrast with the Foundation's goals (while fitting in perfectly well with those of a government).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me too much of the Retcon Stick.

Whenever someone tries to move it, their history is rewritten so that instead of being a Foundation employee, they're D-class personnel with a history of atrocities.

Except they still remember being Foundation personnel and are very upset about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe expunging data was what they were accused of?

If it said that, it would be good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe expunging data was what they were accused of?
Permanently destroying vital information on a scp would mean tests that need to be redone, time and resources that need to be spent all over again, and might even get people killed out of ignorance. That could certianly ruin a researchers reputation in the Foundation.

Take away the brackets and you've got a perfectly valid entry specific to Foundation staff, and it also removes the basis for any accusations of trope use on your part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know that I could pull it off, but it would be interesting to see an article where such a lock were a necessary part of the containment protocols.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup. Although I do find the mental image of a "duel" lock amusing.

"Ugh. We need to go open up the thing again."
"Fuck. Okay, you go grab some D-class, I'll go prep the gladiatorial arena."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think what Drewbear meant is that it should be dual lock, not duel lock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reason for the dual key lock is to prevent personnel from abusing this - you don't want someone actually committing a crime, then sneaking in to open the box and stick their hand in to make it look like they touched the damn thing, then using the security camera footage to suggest that any evidence that the crime they committed was just a result of SCP-1453's effects. By making it so that you need two personnel to open the safe, it (hopefully) would prevent such abuse - the Foundation wouldn't want staff treating the stone like a get-out-of-jail-free card.

So, strictly speaking, the lock is not to protect against SCP-1453's effects; it is to prevent the Foundation from unnecessarily having to sort through as many administrative hoops if one of the less scrupulous staff members is accused of misconduct. As the old saying goes: "locks keep honest people honest."

Edit: Ah. Typo. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I thought the last [DATA EXPUNGED] was, for once, used in more-or-less the right way. It's something where even the EXISTENCE OF EVIDENCE is a bad thing; cognito/infohazard? Vile betrayal beyond "security breach"? Installed a webcam in O5-X's toilet and uploaded the pictures/video to the Foundation intranet? Or, as stated, is something that - even though exonerated - would leave a permanent scar on the person/group's profile and had to be wiped clean in a more unilateral way.

Also, I like the thing itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
equipped with a duel key lock

"We need to open up the box"
"Pistols at dawn it is!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a less interesting version of an idea I already thought was dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally don't approve of the concept that it had to be wiped from the database altogether, but whatever. It's a silly nitpick, I just don't agree. You can expunge the identity but I don't know why you have to expunge the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The magic thing wasn't a very big deal with me, it just didn't seem to fit in with the rest of the mentioned offenses. I do think the pedophilia claim might be able to squeeze in there; there's few things that destroy a person's reputation like their coworkers knowing they want to stick their fingers up a little girl's butt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, but I still think that the article would note that the time of the alleged crime can be either before or after touching the stone, even if the Foundation doesn't know how far into the past it can go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't state any time constraints because there aren't any time constraints, at least not to the Foundation's knowledge. And while the Foundation could conceivably use lots and lots of D-class to figure it out, that seems self-defeating both because D-class are generally criminals (and who knows what they did that they weren't in jail for) and there is no guarantee that the stone would oblige and give useful (non-randomly distributed) data. I don't see a site director approving such testing when tossing the object in a locked box and all but forgetting about it seems a viable solution for containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could be that what the Agent was accused of had to do with SCP-579, which would definitely get the information redacted. Maybe it could be noted that when the SCP frames a Foundation member there's a high chance that the planted evidence will leak sensitive information to people who don't have the clearance to know it, to imply that the [REDACTED] is about sensitive information rather than something horrible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The accusations will be made within one week to six months of touching SCP-1453;

I think the article should note any time constraints on when the crime is supposed to have taken place. I get the impression that the time of the alleged crime can be days, months, or years before the stone is touched, but the article should state this explicitly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<puts on authority hat relating to government redaction>

If something is expunged, it is deleted. That sheet of paper was run through the shredder and all copies were stuck in the furnace. Gone. NOBODY has access to it anymore.

If something is redacted, it just means that only certain people are allowed to know it. I drew a line over the words with a sharpie. The data is still there, even though you can't see it. If you are granted clearance, then you are allowed to read what was censored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, though it reminds me of the brick that causes stupid ass laws. Not sure why, just does. I liked it. Though the list having [DATA EXPUNGED] at the end made me literally face palm at first, but your comments on it bring it to a tolerable point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically because of a combination Eskobar's reasons seven and eight. Also because of the technical difference between redacted (data exists, you just aren't allowed to read it) and expunged (data no longer exists, having been wiped from the database).

The Chaos Insurgency mole was just an example, though that's the sort of thing I have in my head. I have absolutely no problems with someone else deciding that it is something disgusting or uncivilized or whatever. After all, different people have different standards of what would cause someone to lose respect in their eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [DATA EXPUNGED] is (to my knowledge) something removed entirely from the doccumentation (aside from O5 archives) whereas [REDACTED] is a security concern. Just a personal nitpick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. Then why not simply say 'An Agent was accused of being a Chaos Insurgency mole'? You don't have to identify an agent. Or are we trying to expunge that the incident happened at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, that's not what I said.

It could simply be a security concern. Example (which you're welcome to use in your headcanon or not): perhaps Agent B. Arnold is accused of being a Chaos Insurgency mole. That whole incident might have been expunged by Foundation Counterintelligence simply to help close up the security issues, not because it's particularly horrific.

Not everything expunged is horrific. The offense at the end of the article does not have to be something horrific, simply something which would necessarily damage the accused's reputation in his/her community.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, thanks for the compliment on the writing style and tone.

As far as the idea goes, it is something which threatens not your life or limb but your reputation. I, for one, am far more terrified of "adult fears" - things like being falsely accused and subsequently disgraced, or threats to my children (once I have children), or being out of work and unable to put food on the table, or any of these sorts of things - as compared to run of the mill "scary monster things."

As to the [DATA EXPUNGED], I responded with my thoughts on that above (simulpost with this one), so I shan't rehash that here.

As far as the charge of magic, and the object's age, the Foundation may well have been aware of both the object and associated folklore for some time (note the comments in the article about the original beliefs that simple possession of the object was sufficient to cause its effect in the second paragraph of the description), there's no reason that the Foundation was necessarily able to obtain it until recently. This is a small gemstone that would fit in your hand. Nothing's to say it didn't spend decades in a shoebox in somebody's attic - I'm sure I'm hardly the only person to have a collection of assorted shiny rocks gathering dust that I got from various places as a kid. Taking the historical accounts of the stone (accounts which are implied by the article to exist in the middle ages and classical era) and then actually finding the damn thing might have taken the Foundation decades - and as a "Safe" object, probably not something as high on the acquisitions priority as something "Keter". If this was in your closet and you'd not touched it in fifteen years since that time Bobby accused you of threatening to punch him back in kindergarten (or whatever might have ruined your kindergarten reputation), then how would the Foundation necessarily know that it was there?

Remember, SCP articles are generally executive summaries. It is not at all uncommon for there to be (never written) supplements, addenda, logs, and all that which are either mentioned or simply implied to exist without appearing on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, basically, what you just said boiled down to 'because it's so horrifying the foundation had to expunge it'. It still bothers me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most horrific? Not necessarily. The list should neither be considered to be in exact chronological order nor in exact descending order of "horrific-ness". The reason [DATA EXPUNGED] (which in my own headcanon is Foundation-related) is last is because, like "causing a Foundation containment breach" it had been added as a recent addition to the list. The Foundation does terrible things, contains things that do worse things, and generally doesn't expunge data just because it's ohmygodwhatisthatitissohorrific!; it expunges things because it has a valid reason to do so. When I wrote this, I intended it as some Foundation-affiliated person was accused of doing something that would have cost them their respect in the organization, which they covered up either 1) because of the potential security breach that the something posed if knowledge of it spread within the Foundation, and/or 2) the Powers That Be at the Foundation realized that whatever this something the fellow was accused of doing he/she didn't actually do and this was an attempt to help protect that person's reputation.

In other words, the Foundation may not care (as much) about rape or murder or whatever, but the [DATA EXPUNGED] is something it does care about enough to redact. While that which is behind the [DATA EXPUNGED] most certainly would destroy the good standing of the accused, there is no guarantee you or I would find it more horrific than rape or murder or whatever.

</author's commentary>

So, I didn't just unthinkingly follow the "put [DATA EXPUNGED] at the end of the list" trope, but you are of course entitled to both think and vote how you like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was just a very boring idea to me. Reminds me way too much of Subtle Killer Morgan Freeman. The writing style and tone were top-notch though — Something that hasn't been seen here in a while. You then had to murder it with the final [EXPUNGED]. Why was that necessary? Cut out the expunged and put in pedophilia, I'm surprised that isn't on there as it is one of the most reputationally detrimental accusations and it is frequently inflicted on the innocent.

Also get rid of magic, because it exposes a plot hole that's present in another part of the article, but you can overlook the other one. I don't think that magic is something that one can be accused of in today's world. You have added that somehow the object has existed for thousands of years, yet there is no real way to prove that, but this would explain the magic idea better. However, if the item has only been under Foundation control since sometime in the 2000s, how would the Foundation be aware of effects of the item in medieval times, or at least when they had witch-hunts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so tired of every time an author decides to include a list, the last thing is always [EXPUNGED]. What are you trying to say? That you saved the most horrific thing for last, and it's so horrific that higher-ups removed it from the document? That you needed an even number of list items, but couldn't conjure up the final object? Is there a hidden rule that every article must [EXPUNGE] something? Someone needs to write an article explaining this before I try to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final expunged bothers me. Other than that, pretty okay.
